title: Assumption visibility: agents must surface guesses, not silently execute them
description: Add a structured assumption-flagging convention to the shared agent base prompt and task output parsing. When an agent infers ambiguous intent or fills in missing parameters, it must emit a tagged line — [ASSUMPTION: <what was inferred>] — before proceeding. The task worker captures and logs these tags separately from the output body, making guess-vs-execution visible in logs without blocking the pipeline.
rationale: The reading note's core finding: agents guess without marking that they're guessing. Users (and orchestrators) see execution, but the underlying action is improvisation. This maps directly to Mira's agents: when a task description is ambiguous, the agent silently picks an interpretation and proceeds. CLAUDE.md Rule 1 (never hallucinate actions) addresses fabricated completions, but there's no corresponding rule for fabricated *intent interpretation*. The assumption tag creates a lightweight friction point — not enough to block, but enough to make guesses visible in logs for reflection and debugging.
diff_description: In prompts.py, append to the base system prompt shared by all agents: 'If you are inferring ambiguous intent or filling in unspecified parameters, emit [ASSUMPTION: <what you inferred and why>] on its own line before acting. Do not silently substitute your interpretation for the user's intent.' In task_worker.py, after receiving agent output, scan for lines matching the pattern r'^\[ASSUMPTION: (.+)\]', extract them, and write them to a separate 'assumptions' key in the task log entry (alongside stdout/stderr/status). This gives the evaluator agent and weekly reflection a structured signal for how often and where agents are guessing.
